docs.title: Welcome to MkDocs
docs.text: For full documentation visit mkdocs.org.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• mkdocs new [dir-name] - Create a new project.
  • mkdocs serve - Start the live-reloading docs server.
  • mkdocs build - Build the documentation site.
  • mkdocs -h - Print help message and exit.

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
docs/
    index.md  # The documentation homepage.
    ...       # Other markdown pages, images and other files.

docs.title: Tasked to Do
docs.text: • Find the top 3 bad-looking websites and choose one for the UI/UX project. The website will be redesigned by us and presented to the class later on.

docs.title: How Did We Do It
docs.text:
1. Went on Google and searched for "worst website design".
  2. Looked through several examples and picked three.
  3. Chose one that fits best and is easiest to redesign.
  4. Showed it to Chris and asked questions about the project.

docs.title: What Website I Chose
docs.text:
I chose the Discount Beds Belfast website.

Here is an image of the website:

docs.title: Why I Chose This
docs.text:
• It looks super messy even with a simple design.
  • The design looks blank and outdated.
  • It provides too much information on one page.

docs.title: Are you working alone or team? Why?
docs.text: • I work with my team because it feels better to share work problems with trusted people.

docs.title: What hope to get out of the classes?
docs.text: • Understand what is a bad web UI/UX design is and point them out.

docs.title: TASK User Testing
docs.text:
• Bring your wireframes on paper
  • All group members must arrive on time

Wireframes should represent your final redesign and include:

  • Actual content (headings) to give users context
  • Actual links

During user testing:

  • You cannot talk to the tester (except to guide tasks)
  • You are only observing and taking notes
  • In groups, one person acts as the facilitator (switching pages if needed)

docs.title: How did we do it
docs.text:
• Our group have 3 people so we decide to switch role (facilicator, observer and tester) after each test.
  • After finish testing, everyone in class and also Crish gave us their opinions and what change to make it better.
  • We note them down to use it for later.

docs.title: To do
docs.text: In 3 weeks time you are going to create a high-fidelity version of your wireframes (figma, equivalent) High Fidelity = existing layout, add typography (fonts), colors, images (not required)

docs.title: Task
docs.text: We were required to choose 3 usability heuristics to focus on and apply them to our high-fidelity wireframes using the following resource

docs.title: What We Did
docs.text:
• Chose 3 usability heuristics to focus on
  • Identified usability issues in our chosen website based on those heuristics
  • Proposed solutions to improve those issues
  • Analyzed how the website could be improved overall
  • Compiled all findings into a shared document

docs.title: What we got out of class
docs.text:
• Gained a better understanding of how usability heuristics apply to real-world designs
  • Identified key usability problems and areas for improvement
  • Developed practical solutions to enhance user experience

docs.title: Task
docs.text: Present our high-fidelity wireframes (from Week 10) to Chris for feedback.

docs.title: How We Completed the Task
docs.text:
1. Created a Figma project and shared access with all team members
  2. Collaborated both in class and outside of class
  3. Designed 9 pages in Figma based on our original wireframes
  4. Applied colors, typography, and materials selected earlier
  5. Tested the design internally before presenting it to Chris

docs.title: Why We Chose Figma
docs.text:
• We learned Figma in our first semester
  • It is a powerful and industry-standard tool for UI/UX design
  • Provides useful built-in features such as:
  • Real-time collaboration
  • Prototyping
  • Easy design iteration

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
• Overall, the design was successful and functional
  • However, there were some issues with:
  • Spacing and alignment
  • These areas need improvement before the final presentation

docs.title: To do
docs.text:
• Refine spacing and layout consistency
  • Finalize the best version of our high-fidelity wireframes
  • Prepare for the Week 15 presentation

docs.title: What I learned and did in ACIT 2811 UX/UI last week
7 usability factors
docs.text:
1. Useful - is a product/service useful
  2. Usable - is a product/service usable
  3. Findable - is a product/service findable
  4. Credible - is a product/service credible
  5. Desirable - is a product/service desirable
  6. Accessible - is a product/service accessible
  7. Valuable - is a product/service valuable

docs.title: User-Centered Design
docs.text:
• Context: which the user is using the product or service
  • User Testing: feedback to validate that our assumptions are correct
  • Surveys
  • Interviews

Iterative set of steps to solve problems that are specific to products or services for users

  1. Understand the context of use (how, where, why are people using the product/service)
  2. Specify requirements (what does the product need to do)
  3. Design solutions (to the problems that users are facing)
  4. Evaluate solutions (test, get feedback)

docs.title: Five-stage methodology
docs.text:
1. Empathize - identify problems by people/users
  2. Define - analyzing/defining/articulating the problems from step 1
  3. Ideate - brainstorming, generating as many solutions as possible (non-judgemental)
  4. Prototype - pick the best solutions and prototype them
  5. Test - use the prototype to validate assumptions and get feedback

docs.title: First activity: Empathize
docs.text:
Imagine that you are a prospective student at BCIT and identify a challenge they might face (different from your own)

  • Define - one or two specific problems they would face
  • Ideate - brainstorm a few solutions
  • Prototype - define a possible prototype
  • Test - define how you can test the prototype

docs.title: Second activity
docs.text:
Our group was assigned two factors. We had to:

  1. Give an example of a product/service that DOES fulfill a factor and one that DOES NOT
  2. Explain how those products/services could be improved
  3. Our example:
  4. Plastic bottle → accessible but not valuable (everywhere but low value)
  5. Phone → accessible and valuable (used by almost everyone)

Group Activity

docs.title: What I learned and did in ACIT 2811 UX/UI last week
Group Activity: Personas
docs.text:
A persona is a description of a possible/ideal user of a product/service. We create personas:

  • To build empathy
  • To identify user characteristics
  • To identify user behaviours
  • To identify user goals

docs.title: Create two personas (1 page each)
docs.text:
• Photo
  • Name
  • Age
  • Occupation
  • Needs & Goals
  • Pain Points (difficulties)

docs.title: Group Activity: User Stories
docs.text:
TASK: Personas identify user characteristics, behaviours, and goals User Stories identify features (technical features of a product/service that need to be built)

User Stories make up a list of tasks to ensure that, at the end of the project, everything is built.

For each of your two personas, create three User Stories.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text: "As a , I want to , so that I can ___"

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
As a prospective student, I want to learn about the admission process, so that I can start a program

  • These must be written from the perspective of your Personas

docs.title: Group Activity: User Scenarios
docs.text: A User Scenario is a technical description of the steps required to complete one of your user stories

docs.title: USER STORY
docs.text: As a medical director, I want to download spec sheets so that I can verify compliance details

docs.title: USER SCENARIO
docs.text:
1. Start at the home page
  2. Click the downloads link in the menu
  3. On the downloads page, filter documents
  4. Click the file link

docs.title: Clarify the 5 Ws
docs.text: Five Ws - Who — Who is using the site - What — What is the site offering/doing, what should the main focus be - Where — Online - When — ASAP, later? - Why — Why create/redesign the site

docs.title: Feature List
docs.text:
Scope — what are you going to do/implement for this website redesign

Examples: - Update branding (colors, typography) - Redesign home page layout - Redesign specific page layouts - Redesign menu

docs.title: Ideation
docs.text:
Generate as many possible solutions to the problem as you can (non-judgemental)

Techniques - Brainstorming - Worst Possible Idea - Crazy 8s (quick wireframe sketches)

docs.title: Information Architecture (IA)
docs.text:
Organizing and labelling content to help users find information

Includes: - Navigation (menu type, number of menus, hierarchy) - Deep → few top-level links, many nested links - Shallow → many top-level links, fewer nested links - Labelling (language/style used) - Content inventory (list of all pages/content) - Sitemap (visual structure of content) - Wireframes (layout sketches of pages)

docs.title: Group Exercise
docs.text:
As a group, categorize the following into 3–5 top-level menu headings:

  • Accessibility
  • Audition Info
  • Contact Us
  • Current Shows
  • Donate
  • Getting Here
  • Join Our Team
  • Musical Theatre Camp
  • Our Story
  • Past Shows
  • Ticket Information
  • Venue Information
  • Visitor Questions (FAQ)
  • Volunteering

docs.title: Information Architecture
docs.text: This week focused on how to organize and structure content so users can easily navigate and understand a website or application.

docs.title: Navigation Systems
docs.text:
Navigation systems allow users to move through a website or app.

  • Includes menus, links, and navigation bars
  • Should be simple and consistent
  • Helps users find information quickly

docs.title: Labelling Systems
docs.text:
Labelling systems refer to the names used for menus, buttons, and sections.

  • Should be clear and easy to understand
  • Uses familiar and common terms
  • Avoids confusing or vague wording

docs.title: Sitemap
docs.text:
A sitemap is a visual structure of all pages in a website or application.

  • Shows how pages are connected
  • Helps organize content
  • Supports better navigation design

docs.title: What we got out of class
docs.text:
• Understood the importance of clear navigation systems
  • Improved ability to label sections in a user-friendly way

docs.title: What I learned and did in ACIT 2811 UX/UI last week
Prototyping
docs.text: This week focused on understanding how to plan and visualize a design before building it.

docs.title: User Flows
docs.text:
User flows show the steps a user takes to complete a task in an app or website.

  • Focus on the user’s journey
  • Helps identify how users move between pages
  • Useful for improving usability

docs.title: User Flow Diagrams
docs.text:
User flow diagrams are visual representations of how users navigate through a system.

  • Use shapes and arrows to show steps
  • Show decisions and different paths
  • Help understand user behavior

docs.title: Low-Fidelity Mockups
docs.text:
Low-fidelity mockups are basic visual representations of a design.

  • Simple and quick to create
  • Usually black and white
  • Focus on structure and functionality
  • Not detailed or styled

docs.title: What we got out of class
docs.text:
• Learned how to plan designs before starting development
  • Understood how users interact with an application step by step

docs.title: Subtractive Color (Physical Media)
docs.text:
• Starts with white
  • White reflects all colors in the spectrum
  • Adding pigment (e.g., markers, paint) removes/subtracts light

Example: - Yellow + Blue = Green

docs.title: Additive Color (Digital Screens)
docs.text:
• Starts with black
  • Uses RGB (Red, Green, Blue) as primary colors
  • Colors are created by adding light

Secondary Colors: - Cyan - Magenta - Yellow

docs.title: Color Harmonies & UI Requirements
docs.text: For high-fidelity wireframes, the design must include:

docs.title: Color Scheme
docs.text:
• Primary Color → Most frequently used
  • Secondary Color → Supporting color
  • Highlight Color → Used sparingly for emphasis

Rules: - Do NOT include black or white as part of the 3-color scheme (but still use them in the design) - Must follow a valid color harmony - Cannot use: - Analogous - Monochromatic

docs.title: Accessibility (IMPORTANT)
docs.text:
• Must meet WCAG AA contrast standards

  • Use: https://webaim.org/resources/contrastchecker/

  • Do NOT rely only on color to show meaning (important for color-blind users)

Use: - Icons - Text labels - Visual indicators

docs.title: Color & Branding
docs.text:
• Colors should reflect the purpose and audience
  • Example:
  • Blue → Trust / Professional
  • Red → Urgency / Energy
  • Green → Nature / Growth

docs.title: Font Basics
docs.text: • Font-family = Typeface (e.g., Helvetica, Arial)

docs.title: Display Fonts (Headings)
docs.text:
• More expressive
  • Used for titles and emphasis

docs.title: Body Fonts
docs.text:
• Simple and readable
  • Used for paragraphs and content

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use 2 fonts only:
  • 1 for headings

  • 1 for body text

  • Choose fonts with a similar style/feel

  • Common pairing: Serif + Sans-serif

docs.title: Avoid
docs.text:
• Comic Sans
  • Papyrus
  • Cursive fonts

docs.title: What me and my team did:
docs.text:
• We use Metrophobic and Playsen Sans for the Typography.
  • Metrophobic

  • Playsen Sans

  • We chose these color because it bring a relax and sleepy feeling which is fit our standard.
